name: Nott
shortDescription: Nott is the Norse personification of night, daughter of a giant, and mother of Day. She rides her horse Hrimfaxi across the sky, bringing darkness.
description: In Norse mythology, Nott (Old Norse for 'Night') is the personification of night. She is described in both the Poetic Edda and the Prose Edda. According to the Prose Edda, Nott is the daughter of the giant Narfi or Norfi and is described as dark and swarthy. She is married three times: first to Naglfari, with whom she had a son named Aud; second to Annar, with whom she had a daughter named Jord (Earth); and third to Dellingr, with whom she had a son named Dagr (Day), who is bright and fair. Odin placed Nott and Dagr in the sky to ride around the earth every 24 hours, each with their own horse. Nott rides Hrimfaxi, whose frost-covered mane brings dew to the earth each morning.
subtitle: Dark Mother of Night, Rider of Hrimfaxi
role: To bring night to the world and traverse the sky with her horse.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Nott's parentage is given in the Prose Edda, specifically in the Gylfaginning section. She is described as the daughter of the giant Narfi or Norfi. Her lineage connects her to the primordial giants, beings of immense power and ancient origins. Her three marriages each resulted in offspring who represent different aspects of the cosmos. Her union with Dellingr, representing dawn or brightness, produced Dagr, symbolizing the natural cycle of day and night. The contrasting natures of Nott and Dagr highlight the duality inherent in the Norse worldview. The establishment of their celestial journey by Odin emphasizes the structured order of the cosmos.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Nott's role is crucial in the Norse understanding of time and the cosmos. She is not merely a passive representation of night but an active force that drives the daily cycle. Her journey across the sky on Hrimfaxi, followed by Dagr on Skinfaxi, reinforces the cyclical nature of time and the balance between darkness and light. This daily rhythm was essential for agricultural practices, navigation, and the overall understanding of the world. Nott's existence provides a mythological explanation for the natural phenomenon of nightfall, imbuing it with a sense of divine purpose.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Nott symbolizes the mystery, rest, and potential dangers associated with darkness. Night is often seen as a time for introspection, dreams, and the unknown. While Dagr represents clarity and activity, Nott embodies the opposite qualities. Her dark complexion and giant lineage further emphasize her connection to the primordial and untamed aspects of the world. However, night is not inherently evil in Norse mythology; it is a necessary part of the cosmic balance. Nott's presence ensures that the world experiences periods of rest and rejuvenation, allowing for the continuation of life. She represents the cyclical nature of existence and the importance of embracing both light and darkness.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Nott's influence in modern culture is less direct compared to other Norse deities like Thor or Odin. However, she remains a figure of interest in neopaganism and Norse-inspired fiction. Her name and imagery are sometimes used in art, literature, and music to evoke themes of darkness, mystery, and the power of the night. Her story serves as a reminder of the importance of balance and the interconnectedness of opposing forces. As interest in Norse mythology continues to grow, Nott's role as the personification of night ensures her continued presence in contemporary interpretations of these ancient myths.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A 13th-century Icelandic prose work that retells and interprets many Norse myths.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems that are a primary source for Norse mythology.

associations.name: Hrimfaxi
associations.significance: Nott's horse, whose frosty mane brings dew to the earth.

associations.name: Darkness
associations.significance: Nott is the embodiment of darkness and night.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Nyx
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: The Greek goddess of the night, similar in role and representing darkness.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Noktī
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: The Roman goddess of the night, equivalent to the Greek Nyx.

companions.name: Dagr
companions.description: The personification of Day, son of Nott and Dellingr, who follows her across the sky.

companions.name: Jord
companions.description: The personification of Earth, daughter of Nott and Annar.

cultural.modernLegacy: Nott is a figure referenced in modern neopaganism and is also used in literature, art, and music to evoke themes of night and darkness.
